ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for fish tikka; mapped to 'fish food product' as the closest defensible ancestor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for fish tikka; A00GJ (Fish, cooked) is the closest defensible broader category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found specifically for fish tikka; left null to maintain ontology honesty.

main_ingredients: white fish (e.g. basa, tilapia, or similar firm-fleshed fish)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of fish tikka consists of approximately 4 to 5 boneless fish chunks weighing around 200 g in total, served with mint chutney and sliced onions.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods — Fish Tikka / Tandoori Fish (grilled, marinated in spiced yoghurt)
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017 edition) provides values for tandoori fish / grilled spiced fish. Fish tikka is a closely related preparation — boneless fish marinated in spiced yoghurt and grilled in a tandoor. Values are adapted from the ICMR-NIN grilled/tandoori fish entry and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for cooked fish (tilapia/basa class) to validate protein and fat ranges. The adaptation is documented here; confidence is set to medium accordingly.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — protein-dominant grilled fish dishes; analogous estimate from tandoori chicken and grilled fish entries
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for fish tikka in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI is estimated at 30 (low) based on the following analogous reasoning: (1) Fish tikka is a high-protein, low-carbohydrate grilled dish with minimal starch; (2) The University of Sydney GI Database lists tandoori chicken (a structurally analogous marinated, tandoor-grilled protein dish) with GI values in the 25–35 range; (3) Grilled fish dishes with spiced yoghurt marinades are consistently low-GI due to protein and fat content slowing gastric emptying; (4) Carbohydrate content per 100 g is approximately 3.2 g, yielding a glycemic load per 200 g serving of approximately 1.9 g, which is negligible. Estimation basis is fully documented.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from marinade spices and salt
hypertension.guidance: Fish tikka contains approximately 420 mg sodium per 100 g (840 mg per typical 200 g serving), which represents about 37% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2300 mg; individuals with hypertension should request reduced-salt preparation and limit portion size to one serving.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content and high protein with low glycemic index
type2_diabetes.guidance: Fish tikka has a low glycemic index (estimated GI ~30) and contains only approximately 3.2 g carbohydrate per 100 g, making it a suitable high-protein, low-glycaemic option for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without high-carbohydrate accompaniments such as naan or rice.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Lean fish protein with moderate unsaturated fat and omega-3 content
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Fish tikka provides lean protein with a favourable unsaturated-to-saturated fat ratio and omega-3 fatty acids from the fish, supporting cardiovascular health; however, the moderate sodium content warrants attention for individuals with heart failure or salt-sensitive hypertension.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Fish Tikka is a classic North Indian tandoor-grilled dish consisting of boneless chunks of firm white fish — typically basa, tilapia, or similar varieties — marinated in a spiced yoghurt mixture containing cumin, garam masala, turmeric, chilli powder, ginger, garlic, and lemon juice. The marinated fish is skewered and cooked at high heat in a clay tandoor oven, producing a lightly charred exterior with a moist, tender interior. The dish is characterised by its smoky aroma, vibrant orange-red colour from the spice marinade, and a mildly spiced, tangy flavour profile. Fish tikka is a high-protein, low-carbohydrate dish with approximately 20.5 g protein and only 3.2 g carbohydrate per 100 g, making it nutritionally suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or following low-glycaemic diets. Its low glycemic index (estimated ~30) and lean protein content also support cardiovascular health. In Singapore, fish tikka is commonly served at North Indian restaurants as a starter or main course, accompanied by mint chutney, sliced onions, and lemon wedges. It is naturally gluten-free and halal-certified in most restaurant contexts. The dish is culturally significant as part of the broader tandoori cooking tradition originating in the Punjab region of the Indian subcontinent.
common_user_queries: How many calories in fish tikka?
Is fish tikka good for diabetics?
Is fish tikka high in protein?
What fish is used in fish tikka?
Fish tikka vs chicken tikka nutrition

confidence_reason: No exact fish tikka entry was found in ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods as a standalone dish. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN grilled/tandoori fish entries and cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy cooked fish values. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using structurally similar tandoor-grilled protein dishes. Confidence is set to medium because the source match is adapted rather than exact.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN (2017) grilled/tandoori fish data and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (cooked tilapia/basa class) to validate macro ranges. No single approved source provides an exact fish tikka entry. 2. GI is an analogous estimate (GI ~30, low) based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for structurally similar tandoor-grilled protein dishes; no direct GI measurement for fish tikka was found in the approved GI database. 3. Sodium content (420 mg/100 g) reflects typical restaurant-style preparation with salt in the marinade; home-prepared versions may have lower sodium. 4. The fish species used varies by restaurant; basa and tilapia are most common in Singapore's North Indian restaurant context. 5. trans_fat_g is reported as 0.0 — confirmed negligible/trace in grilled fish preparations per USDA SR Legacy; this is a confirmed trace value consistent with the cooking method. 6. The food_name has been normalised from 'Fish Tikka (Chef Recommendation)' to the canonical form 'Fish Tikka'; the promotional qualifier 'Chef Recommendation' is preserved in aliases.
